note: Verbatim phrases of the locked narration, spoken order, split at the script's break tags and sentence boundaries. Block windows are the blocks' MEASURED positions in the joined master: Σ(audio+0.4) predicts 291.892s but the ffprobed master is 291.318s (0.57s off, past the ±0.3s gate), so each block start is its true onset found by FFT normalized cross-correlation of sNN.mp3 against the joined master (8 kHz mono PCM); every segment matched at ncc=1.000, implied inter-block gaps ~0.36s. Final end = ffprobed master duration 291.318s.

phrases.text: Open Settings, then open Services.
This is the catalog.
Everything a site sells lives here as a row: a wash, an add-on, a token.
Every price that shows up on the point of sale traces back to this list,
so a pricing question usually ends right where we're standing.
Look down the Services group in the sidebar.
Services is the catalog itself.
The pages beneath it, Service Types, Service Groups, Detail Types, Rewards,
define the vocabulary a service draws on.
You rarely edit those,
but knowing they exist tells you where a stray label on a service actually comes from.
Read the four tiles across the top.
Twenty-four services, twenty-one active, six types, average price just under fifteen dollars.
It's a health snapshot for the whole catalog, not one site.
And take the counts loosely:
in this demo the tile totals and the row totals don't always agree.
Two cards show the catalog's shape.
The trend line is the count over the last year, ticking up a few percent.
The status card is the one to remember:
active means it sells, draft means it isn't live yet,
and inactive means it's retired but kept for the history.
Most of the catalog is active.
Now the mix by type.
Washes are the biggest slice, then add-ons, then detail work.
That top slice is what we open next,
because a wash package is where the per-site pricing story really shows up.
Drop down to the Services List.
This is the real catalog table.
New Service sits top-right, and it's how an admin adds a wash.
We leave it alone:
creating a service is production work, not something you do from a support seat.
Read the columns.
Description, code, type, price, active.
Watch the code column especially.
That's the controller code,
the value that wires the service to the point of sale and the tunnel controller.
When a button rings up the wrong wash, the code is usually the first thing you check.
Look at the rows.
On a live tenant this fills with the migrated catalog: wash packages, add-ons, token sales.
The demo is quirkier, it often loads one fixed sample when you open a service.
If you ever see a failed-to-fetch message instead of rows,
that's the data service dropping out, not an empty catalog.
There's a search box for a reason.
A full catalog runs a couple hundred rows, so you type the service name rather than scroll.
Filters narrow it further.
We won't run one today;
just know it's the fast way to find a single wash.
Down at the bottom, the page controls.
Twenty-five, fifty, or a hundred rows at a time.
Small thing, but on a big catalog bumping it to a hundred saves a lot of clicking.
Open a wash and read the detail.
On the verified pass a service opened onto two tabs, Service Main and Active Locations,
with Back, Delete, and Edit across the top.
Heads up: in this capture the detail page wouldn't load,
so treat what follows as the layout we confirmed earlier, parked for a fresh check.
Service Main is where the service is defined.
Eight groups of fields, from basic info to pricing to behavior.
Two earn their keep for support.
Controller code, again, is the point-of-sale mapping.
And count as car decides whether the service adds to the site's car count,
which is why selling a token doesn't fake your traffic numbers.
Switch to Active Locations.
This tab is the whole point of the lesson.
It lists the sites and groups where the service actually sells,
each with a status and a start date.
A service only rings up where it's active here.
So if a wash is missing at one site, this is the first tab you open.
Now click Edit, and watch the little gears.
They sit beside a handful of fields,
the description, the controller code, the amount, and the tax settings.
Hover one and it reads configure per location.
The plain field is the default everywhere.
The gear lets an admin override it for a single site or group.
Click the gear beside the amount.
On the verified pass, nothing opened.
The dialog that's supposed to set a per-site value stayed shut in the demo tenant,
so we park that step and check it on a real configuration.
Point at the gear, explain what it does, and back out.
Never save.
So here's the pattern.
One service, one record.
Active Locations decides where it sells.
The gears decide what it costs and how it's taxed at each site.
That's how the Works can run two dollars more at one wash without a second record anywhere.
You'll see the same shape on discounts next, so it's worth owning now.
